module: Module 6 - Hospital and Drug Store Pharmacy

questions.question: In hospital pharmacy, the Pharmacy and Therapeutics (P&T) Committee is responsible for:
choices: Setting pharmacist salaries
Developing and maintaining the drug formulary and medication use policies
Conducting drug manufacturing inspections
Managing the hospital's outpatient clinic
questions.answer: Developing and maintaining the drug formulary and medication use policies
questions.explanation: The Pharmacy and Therapeutics (P&T) Committee is a multidisciplinary committee (physicians, pharmacists, nurses, administrators) responsible for: developing and maintaining the drug formulary, approving drug use policies, evaluating new drugs, investigating medication errors, and ensuring rational drug use throughout the institution.

questions.question: Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN) must be prepared in which type of environment to maintain sterility?
choices: Standard laboratory bench
Clean room with laminar air flow (LAF) workbench under ISO 5 conditions
Any air-conditioned pharmacy room
Biologics safety cabinet (Class II)
questions.answer: Clean room with laminar air flow (LAF) workbench under ISO 5 conditions
questions.explanation: TPN is a complex IV admixture directly infused into the bloodstream, making sterility critical. It must be prepared in a cleanroom (ISO 7 or better) using a laminar airflow workbench (ISO 5 / Class 100) or, for hazardous drugs, a biological safety cabinet. USP <797> governs sterile compounding standards.

questions.question: The drug distribution system known as 'floor stock' refers to:
choices: Individual patient-specific doses dispensed per order
Bulk supplies of commonly used medications stored on nursing units for immediate use
Medications dispensed via automated dispensing machines only
Emergency crash cart medications exclusively
questions.answer: Bulk supplies of commonly used medications stored on nursing units for immediate use
questions.explanation: Floor stock is a system where nursing units maintain a supply of commonly needed medications. Advantages: immediate availability for urgent needs. Disadvantages: increased risk of medication errors, drug diversion, waste, and poor pharmacist control. Modern hospitals combine floor stock (emergencies) with unit-dose or automated dispensing systems.

questions.question: An Automated Dispensing Cabinet (ADC) in a hospital pharmacy is used to:
choices: Prepare and sterilize IV medications automatically
Control and document access to medications at the point of care, improving accuracy and tracking
Compound sterile preparations
Only store controlled substances
questions.answer: Control and document access to medications at the point of care, improving accuracy and tracking
questions.explanation: ADCs (e.g., Pyxis, Omnicell) are computerized cabinets placed in nursing units. They require user authentication, provide medication access based on patient-specific orders, maintain a complete access log, reduce dispensing errors, improve controlled drug accountability, and integrate with pharmacy information systems.

questions.question: A 'stat' medication order in hospital pharmacy means:
choices: The medication is needed within 24 hours
The medication is needed only when required (PRN)
The medication is required immediately or urgently
The medication is part of the regular daily order
questions.answer: The medication is required immediately or urgently
questions.explanation: 'STAT' (from Latin: statim = immediately) means the medication must be dispensed and administered immediately. Pharmacy standards typically require STAT medications to be dispensed within 15-30 minutes. Other order types: 'ASAP' (as soon as possible), 'routine' (within 2-4 hours), 'PRN' (as needed).

questions.question: Drug incompatibility in IV admixtures can be detected by which observation?
choices: Change in pH of the solution
Precipitation, color change, cloudiness, or gas formation in the IV bag or syringe
Increased viscosity of the solution
All of the above can indicate incompatibility
questions.answer: All of the above can indicate incompatibility
questions.explanation: Physical incompatibilities are visible: precipitation (e.g., diazepam in normal saline), color change (oxidation of certain vitamins), cloudiness (amphotericin B mixing), or effervescence. Chemical incompatibilities may not be visible. pH change can catalyze degradation. IV compatibility references (King Guide, Trissel's) should always be consulted.

questions.question: The role of the clinical pharmacist in hospital practice includes all EXCEPT:
choices: Conducting medication reconciliation
Performing drug use evaluations and audits
Prescribing medications independently without physician oversight
Providing drug information to healthcare teams
questions.answer: Prescribing medications independently without physician oversight
questions.explanation: Clinical pharmacists in the Philippines are medication management experts who: perform medication reconciliation, monitor drug therapy, identify and resolve drug therapy problems, counsel patients, provide drug information, participate in rounds, and conduct medication reviews. Prescribing authority in the Philippines remains primarily with licensed physicians.

questions.question: In community pharmacy, the maximum number of refills for a Schedule IV (dangerous drug) prescription is generally:
choices: Unlimited refills for 1 year
Maximum 3 refills
Maximum 5 refills in 6 months
No refills are permitted; a new prescription is required each time
questions.answer: No refills are permitted; a new prescription is required each time
questions.explanation: Under Philippine dangerous drugs regulations, prescriptions for controlled substances (Schedule II/III drugs per RA 9165) typically cannot be refilled; a new prescription is required for each dispensing. This is to prevent misuse. For Schedule IV/V (maintenance medications), regulations may allow limited refills with restrictions.

questions.question: Pharmaceutical waste disposal must comply with regulations because:
choices: Expired drugs can be repackaged and resold
Improper disposal causes environmental contamination and public health risks
Unused drugs must be returned to manufacturers only
All expired drugs are non-toxic
questions.answer: Improper disposal causes environmental contamination and public health risks
questions.explanation: Pharmaceutical waste (expired drugs, unused medications, contaminated materials) must be disposed of according to RA 9275 (Philippine Clean Water Act), DENR regulations, and DOH guidelines. Methods: incineration (for hazardous), encapsulation, and licensed waste collectors. Improper disposal contaminates water supplies and soil.

questions.question: The 'first-in, first-out' (FIFO) principle in pharmacy inventory management means:
choices: The newest stock is dispensed first
Stock with the earliest expiration date is dispensed first
The most expensive drugs are dispensed first
The drug with the highest demand is restocked first
questions.answer: Stock with the earliest expiration date is dispensed first
questions.explanation: FIFO ensures that older inventory (with earlier expiry dates) is used before newer stock. When restocking, new products are placed behind existing stock. This minimizes expired drug waste and ensures patients receive medications with adequate shelf life. Critical for refrigerated items and short-shelf-life products.

questions.question: A 'crash cart' (emergency medicine cart) in a hospital typically contains which critical medication?
choices: Oral antibiotics and vitamins
Epinephrine, atropine, amiodarone, and other cardiac emergency drugs
Antihypertensive maintenance medications
Nutritional supplements and IV fluids only
questions.answer: Epinephrine, atropine, amiodarone, and other cardiac emergency drugs
questions.explanation: The crash cart (code cart) contains medications for cardiac arrest and other emergencies: epinephrine (1:10,000), atropine, amiodarone, lidocaine, adenosine, calcium chloride, sodium bicarbonate, vasopressin, and dextrose 50%. Also contains defibrillator, airway supplies, and IV equipment. Checked regularly for completeness and expiry.

questions.question: The BeyondUse Date (BUD) of a compounded sterile preparation is different from an expiration date because the BUD:
choices: Is set by the manufacturer based on stability studies
Is assigned based on the time and conditions of compounding, not long-term stability data
Is always longer than the expiration date
Applies only to non-sterile preparations
questions.answer: Is assigned based on the time and conditions of compounding, not long-term stability data
questions.explanation: BUD is the date/time after which a compounded preparation shall not be used. It is based on risk category (low, medium, high-risk sterile preparations per USP <797>) and storage conditions, not extensive stability testing. It is typically much shorter than manufacturer expiration dates. BUD protects sterility and chemical stability.

questions.question: The primary purpose of the Formulary Management System in a hospital is to:
choices: Limit all patients to a single standard treatment regardless of individual needs
Optimize drug use through evidence-based selection, standardization, and cost management
Allow each physician to prescribe only their preferred drugs
Satisfy pharmaceutical company requests for product inclusion
questions.answer: Optimize drug use through evidence-based selection, standardization, and cost management
questions.explanation: Formulary management selects the safest, most effective, and most cost-effective medications for the institution. It reduces drug variability, supports therapeutic standardization, decreases inventory costs, and promotes rational prescribing. The P&T Committee periodically reviews the formulary based on new evidence.

questions.question: In Philippine drug store operations, which section or area must be supervised directly by a registered pharmacist?
choices: The consumer goods section
The prescription and dangerous drug dispensing area
The cosmetics section only
The snacks and beverages section
questions.answer: The prescription and dangerous drug dispensing area
questions.explanation: RA 5921 requires a registered pharmacist to personally supervise all dispensing of prescription medications and controlled substances. The pharmacist must be physically present during prescription dispensing. Other areas (OTC, consumer goods) may be managed by trained staff, but the pharmacy dispensing area requires direct pharmacist supervision.

questions.question: Drug storage in a hospital pharmacy requires that thermolabile medications (vaccines, insulin, some biologics) be stored at:
choices: Room temperature (15-30°C)
Refrigerator temperature (2-8°C)
Freezer temperature (-20°C or below)
Incubator temperature (37°C)
questions.answer: Refrigerator temperature (2-8°C)
questions.explanation: Thermolabile medications must be stored in pharmaceutical refrigerators maintaining 2-8°C. Examples: vaccines (MMR, DPT, Hepatitis B), insulin, some biological products (erythropoietin, IV immunoglobulin). Freezer (-20°C): oral polio vaccine, varicella vaccine. Temperature logs and alarms are required for compliant cold chain management.

questions.question: Aseptic technique in IV admixture preparation includes all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Wearing sterile gloves and mask
Working in a laminar airflow workbench
Shaking the IV bag vigorously to mix contents
Disinfecting vial tops and ampule necks with 70% isopropyl alcohol
questions.answer: Shaking the IV bag vigorously to mix contents
questions.explanation: Aseptic technique principles: work in ISO 5 environment, wear PPE (sterile gloves, mask, gown), disinfect all surfaces and container tops with 70% isopropyl alcohol, minimize exposure to non-sterile surfaces, do not touch critical zones. Vigorous shaking is NOT appropriate — it can introduce air bubbles and potentially cause foaming or degradation of sensitive biologics.

questions.question: A drug information service in a hospital pharmacy provides:
choices: Marketing information about new drugs to physicians
Evidence-based, unbiased drug information to healthcare professionals and patients for clinical decision-making
Drug sample distribution to clinical units
Only information about generic drug substitutions
questions.answer: Evidence-based, unbiased drug information to healthcare professionals and patients for clinical decision-making
questions.explanation: Hospital pharmacy drug information services provide accurate, current, evidence-based, unbiased drug information: drug interactions, dosing guidelines, adverse effects, therapeutic alternatives, and pharmacokinetics. Resources include: Micromedex, Lexicomp, Clinical Pharmacology, primary literature. Distinct from pharmaceutical company promotional material.

questions.question: The medication use process in a hospital includes which sequential steps?
choices: Manufacture → Prescribing → Dispensing
Prescribing → Transcribing → Dispensing → Administering → Monitoring
Storage → Manufacturing → Administering → Billing
Dispensing → Prescribing → Monitoring → Transcribing
questions.answer: Prescribing → Transcribing → Dispensing → Administering → Monitoring
questions.explanation: The medication use process: Prescribing (physician writes order) → Transcribing (pharmacist/nurse transcribes to MAR) → Dispensing (pharmacist prepares and verifies) → Administering (nurse gives medication) → Monitoring (pharmacist/nurse monitors for response and side effects). Errors can occur at any step.

questions.question: In drug store management, 'dead stock' refers to:
choices: Expired medications requiring disposal
Slow-moving or non-moving inventory that ties up capital without generating sales
Controlled substances that cannot be returned
Recalled products awaiting pickup
questions.answer: Slow-moving or non-moving inventory that ties up capital without generating sales
questions.explanation: Dead stock is inventory that does not sell or moves very slowly, tying up capital and storage space. Management strategies: identify slow-moving items, reduce order quantities, promote discounts, return to distributor if possible, avoid ordering in excess. ABC analysis categorizes drugs by sales volume and value to optimize inventory.

questions.question: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM) involves measuring drug plasma levels to:
choices: Determine whether the patient is taking their medication
Optimize drug therapy by ensuring levels are in the therapeutic range, especially for NTI drugs
Check for drug abuse
Determine drug formulation quality
questions.answer: Optimize drug therapy by ensuring levels are in the therapeutic range, especially for NTI drugs
questions.explanation: TDM measures drug plasma concentrations to individualize dosing. Critical for narrow therapeutic index drugs: digoxin, phenytoin, lithium, vancomycin, aminoglycosides, cyclosporine, theophylline. Samples are timed precisely (trough, peak) relative to dosing. The clinical pharmacist interprets results and recommends dose adjustments.

questions.question: A 'Type A' drug reaction (adverse drug reaction) is characterized as:
choices: Idiosyncratic, unpredictable, not dose-related
Augmented pharmacological effects, predictable, dose-related, and the most common type
Delayed hypersensitivity reactions
Drug withdrawal reactions
questions.answer: Augmented pharmacological effects, predictable, dose-related, and the most common type
questions.explanation: Type A (Augmented) reactions are exaggerated but predictable pharmacological effects. They are dose-dependent, common (account for ~80% of ADRs), and manageable by dose reduction. Examples: hypoglycemia from insulin, bleeding from warfarin, constipation from opioids. Type B (Bizarre) reactions are idiosyncratic and unpredictable (e.g., penicillin anaphylaxis).

questions.question: The purpose of 'batch manufacturing records' in pharmaceutical manufacturing is to:
choices: Track sales data for marketing purposes
Document all production steps, materials, and quality tests for each batch to ensure reproducibility and traceability
Record employee work hours
Calculate manufacturing costs only
questions.answer: Document all production steps, materials, and quality tests for each batch to ensure reproducibility and traceability
questions.explanation: Batch Manufacturing Records (BMRs) document every step in producing a pharmaceutical batch: raw materials used, quantities, equipment, processing parameters, in-process tests, yields, and QC release data. They ensure GMP compliance, allow batch traceability, support product recalls, and are legally required for all pharmaceutical products.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key function of the hospital pharmacy's drug information center?
choices: Advertising new drugs to patients
Providing evidence-based responses to drug information queries from healthcare providers
Selling prescription medications directly to patients
Managing hospital finances
questions.answer: Providing evidence-based responses to drug information queries from healthcare providers
questions.explanation: Hospital drug information centers respond to queries from physicians, nurses, and other healthcare professionals regarding: drug dosing, interactions, adverse effects, pharmacokinetics, therapeutic alternatives, and clinical guidelines. They use tertiary (Micromedex), secondary (PubMed), and primary (original research) literature to provide unbiased, evidence-based responses.

questions.question: In a community pharmacy setting, 'patient counseling' for a newly dispensed prescription should include at minimum:
choices: Only the drug's brand name and price
Drug name, indication, dosage, administration instructions, common side effects, storage, and when to contact a doctor
Only the administration route
A full pharmacology lecture on the drug's mechanism
questions.answer: Drug name, indication, dosage, administration instructions, common side effects, storage, and when to contact a doctor
questions.explanation: Comprehensive patient counseling per Good Pharmacy Practice includes: drug name (brand and generic), indication, dose and schedule, route of administration, what to do if a dose is missed, common/important side effects, drug-food/drug-drug interactions, storage instructions, and warning signs requiring medical attention.

questions.question: Centralized IV admixture preparation in hospital pharmacy is preferred over ward-level preparation because it provides:
choices: Faster preparation times
Greater sterility assurance, pharmacist oversight, standardization, and cost control
Less documentation requirements
More convenient access for nurses
questions.answer: Greater sterility assurance, pharmacist oversight, standardization, and cost control
questions.explanation: Centralized IV admixture preparation (CIVA) in the hospital pharmacy ensures: sterility through cleanroom/LAF workbench preparation, pharmacist verification of orders, standardized concentrations, waste reduction, cost savings, and pharmacokinetic monitoring. Ward-level preparation carries higher contamination risk and is less efficiently controlled.

questions.question: Good Distribution Practice (GDP) for pharmaceutical products requires that during transportation, drugs must be:
choices: Transported at any available temperature to reduce cost
Protected from temperature excursions, contamination, damage, and unauthorized access
Transported only by licensed pharmacists
Delivered directly by the manufacturer to patients
questions.answer: Protected from temperature excursions, contamination, damage, and unauthorized access
questions.explanation: GDP guidelines (WHO, ICH) require that pharmaceutical distribution maintains drug quality by: maintaining required temperature ranges (cold chain for thermolabile products), protecting from contamination and physical damage, ensuring proper labeling and documentation, and preventing diversion or theft. Temperature monitoring during transport is mandatory.

questions.question: An 'on-call' pharmacist in a hospital is responsible for:
choices: Handling all administrative duties during business hours
Responding to urgent pharmacy service needs after hours, including emergency drug dispensing and IV preparation
Conducting staff performance reviews
Ordering new pharmaceutical products only
questions.answer: Responding to urgent pharmacy service needs after hours, including emergency drug dispensing and IV preparation
questions.explanation: On-call pharmacists ensure 24-hour pharmacy coverage for emergencies. Responsibilities include: dispensing urgent medications, preparing IV admixtures for unstable patients, providing drug information for emergency situations, managing code cart medications, and addressing after-hours controlled substance requirements.

questions.question: The 'turn-over rate' in drug store inventory management measures:
choices: The number of customers served per day
How many times the average inventory is sold and replaced over a specific period
The percentage of expired products
Annual revenue from prescription sales
questions.answer: How many times the average inventory is sold and replaced over a specific period
questions.explanation: Inventory turnover rate = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory Value. A higher turnover rate means faster-moving stock (more efficient inventory, less capital tied up). Target turnover rates vary but typically 6-12 times per year for a community pharmacy. Slow turnover leads to expiry losses and cash flow problems.

questions.question: Drug recall in the Philippines is coordinated by the FDA and may be classified as Class I, II, or III. A Class I recall involves:
choices: A minor labeling defect with no safety concern
A product that is unlikely to cause adverse health consequences
A product where there is reasonable probability of causing serious health consequences or death
questions.answer: A product where there is reasonable probability of causing serious health consequences or death
questions.explanation: Class I recall: most serious — product causes or has reasonable probability of causing serious adverse health consequences or death (e.g., contaminated injectable with lethal bacteria). Class II: product may cause temporary adverse health consequences, or remote probability of serious harm. Class III: unlikely to cause health harm (minor defect).

questions.question: In compounding, the difference between 'compounding' and 'manufacturing' is that compounding is:
choices: Identical to large-scale manufacturing processes
Preparing individualized or small quantities of drug products for specific patient needs based on a valid prescription
Restricted to hospital inpatient use only
Only done by pharmaceutical companies
questions.answer: Preparing individualized or small quantities of drug products for specific patient needs based on a valid prescription
questions.explanation: Compounding prepares customized medications for individual patients per a valid prescription when commercially available products are unavailable or unsuitable (e.g., pediatric suspensions, topical preparations, allergen-free formulations). Manufacturing produces large commercial batches. Compounding is regulated differently from manufacturing.

questions.question: The 'double-check' procedure for high-alert medications in hospitals requires:
choices: The same pharmacist reviewing the order twice
Two independent healthcare professionals each separately verifying the drug, dose, route, and patient before administration
The nurse to check the drug price twice
Verification by two pharmacists only
questions.answer: Two independent healthcare professionals each separately verifying the drug, dose, route, and patient before administration
questions.explanation: Independent double-check: two separate qualified professionals (e.g., pharmacist + nurse, or two pharmacists) independently verify all critical elements (right patient, drug, dose, concentration, rate, route) before preparing or administering a high-alert medication. Required for: chemotherapy, insulin, anticoagulants, concentrated electrolytes.

questions.question: Which IV fluid is an isotonic crystalloid used as a maintenance fluid for most patients?
choices: 50% Dextrose in Water (D50W)
0.9% Sodium Chloride (Normal Saline)
3% Sodium Chloride (Hypertonic Saline)
Sterile Water for Injection
questions.answer: 0.9% Sodium Chloride (Normal Saline)
questions.explanation: 0.9% NaCl (Normal Saline, NS) is isotonic (~308 mOsmol/L) and is the most widely used IV fluid for volume replacement, drug dilution, and medication administration. 0.45% NaCl is hypotonic (half-normal saline). Lactated Ringer's is also isotonic. D50W is used for hypoglycemia emergencies.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of a community pharmacy's required display or posting?
choices: Certificate of Registration from FDA
Pharmacist's license displayed in a conspicuous place
Price list of prescription medications
The pharmacist's medical diagnosis records
questions.answer: The pharmacist's medical diagnosis records
questions.explanation: Philippine regulations require drug stores to display: FDA Certificate of Registration, pharmacist's PRC license, business permit, and price lists of prescription/OTC drugs (per FDA price monitoring). The pharmacist's personal medical records are private and not required to be displayed or disclosed.

questions.question: Enteral nutrition (tube feeding) in hospital pharmacy management refers to:
choices: IV nutrition administration directly into the bloodstream
Nutritional support delivered through the gastrointestinal tract via feeding tubes
Oral nutritional supplements for alert patients
Only amino acid solutions for surgery patients
questions.answer: Nutritional support delivered through the gastrointestinal tract via feeding tubes
questions.explanation: Enteral nutrition (EN) delivers nutrients via the GI tract through tubes (nasogastric, nasoenteric, gastrostomy, jejunostomy). It is preferred over TPN when the GI tract is functional ('if the gut works, use it'). Benefits: maintains gut integrity, lower cost, fewer infections. Hospital pharmacists select formulas, calculate needs, and monitor.

questions.question: A pharmacist notices a drug has been stored at incorrect temperature for an unknown period. The appropriate action is to:
choices: Continue dispensing as normal since the drug looks unchanged
Quarantine the product, document the incident, and contact the manufacturer or quality assurance for guidance
Discard the drug immediately and reorder without documentation
Lower the price of the potentially compromised medication
questions.answer: Quarantine the product, document the incident, and contact the manufacturer or quality assurance for guidance
questions.explanation: Temperature excursions require quarantine of the affected product, documentation (batch numbers, duration of excursion, temperature data), and consultation with the manufacturer or QA team. The manufacturer can advise whether the product is still usable based on stability data. This prevents dispensing of potentially compromised products.

questions.question: The 'par level' in pharmacy inventory management is:
choices: The maximum amount of a drug that can be stored
The minimum quantity of a drug that must be maintained in stock before reordering
The average selling price of a drug
The maximum retail price allowed by law
questions.answer: The minimum quantity of a drug that must be maintained in stock before reordering
questions.explanation: Par level is the minimum acceptable stock level that triggers a reorder. When inventory reaches par level, a purchase order is generated. Setting appropriate par levels prevents stockouts while avoiding excess inventory. Par levels account for usage rates, lead time, and safety stock. ABC analysis helps prioritize which items need close monitoring.

questions.question: Hazardous drugs (antineoplastics, some antivirals) require special handling because they:
choices: Are more expensive than standard medications
Can cause cancer, reproductive toxicity, organ damage, or other serious effects in healthcare workers with occupational exposure
Have a narrow therapeutic index
Must only be dispensed in hospital settings
questions.answer: Can cause cancer, reproductive toxicity, organ damage, or other serious effects in healthcare workers with occupational exposure
questions.explanation: NIOSH-listed hazardous drugs (chemotherapy agents, some antivirals, immunosuppressants, hormones) require: preparation in a Class II BSC/CACI (not standard LAF), PPE (double gloves, gown, face shield), closed-system drug transfer devices, proper waste disposal, and staff training. Exposure can cause carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, and teratogenicity.

questions.question: The 'economic order quantity' (EOQ) formula in pharmacy management helps determine:
choices: The maximum profit from drug sales
The optimal order quantity that minimizes total inventory costs (ordering + holding costs)
The discount available from bulk purchasing
The number of staff needed in a pharmacy
questions.answer: The optimal order quantity that minimizes total inventory costs (ordering + holding costs)
questions.explanation: EOQ = √(2DS/H) where D = annual demand, S = ordering cost per order, H = holding cost per unit per year. It balances ordering costs (cost per order × number of orders) against holding costs (storage, refrigeration, capital tied up). Too small an order = too many orders; too large = high holding costs.

questions.question: Parenteral nutrition is indicated for patients who:
choices: Prefer IV over oral medications
Cannot tolerate enteral feeding due to non-functioning GI tract, or require supplemental nutrition when enteral nutrition is insufficient
Need only short-term hydration
Have mild nausea from oral medications
questions.answer: Cannot tolerate enteral feeding due to non-functioning GI tract, or require supplemental nutrition when enteral nutrition is insufficient
questions.explanation: TPN indications: short bowel syndrome, bowel obstruction, severe malabsorption, intractable vomiting/diarrhea, prolonged ileus, and when enteral access is impossible. Peripheral parenteral nutrition (PPN) is used for short-term when central access is unavailable. TPN via central venous catheter provides complete nutrition support.

questions.question: Cold chain management in a drugstore requires temperature monitoring because:
choices: FDA requires temperature logs only for controlled substances
Temperature excursions can destroy vaccine potency and degrade heat-sensitive biologics irreversibly
Cold storage is a marketing requirement for premium products
All medications degrade at room temperature
questions.answer: Temperature excursions can destroy vaccine potency and degrade heat-sensitive biologics irreversibly
questions.explanation: Many vaccines (inactivated and live attenuated), insulin, biologics (monoclonal antibodies, erythropoietin), and some IV preparations require 2-8°C storage. Freezing destroys some vaccines (Hep B, DPT) and insulin crystalline structure. Overheating denatures proteins and degrades heat-labile drugs. Daily temperature logs are required; alarms alert to excursions.

questions.question: In the context of medication safety, 'root cause analysis' (RCA) is performed after a medication error to:
choices: Identify and punish the individual who made the error
Identify the underlying system factors contributing to the error and develop system-wide solutions
Document the error for insurance purposes only
Determine financial compensation for the patient
questions.answer: Identify the underlying system factors contributing to the error and develop system-wide solutions
questions.explanation: RCA is a structured process to identify WHY an error occurred, looking beyond individual blame to systemic causes (communication failures, unclear labeling, inadequate training, workflow issues). The goal is to improve systems to prevent recurrence. Blame-based cultures discourage error reporting; safety cultures use RCA constructively.

questions.question: A community pharmacy with sales of controlled drugs must maintain a Dangerous Drug Register (DDR) that records:
choices: All drug sales regardless of schedule
Only purchases and not dispensing of dangerous drugs
Each transaction involving dangerous drugs: receipt, dispensing, balance on hand, and prescriber details
Monthly aggregate sales only
questions.answer: Each transaction involving dangerous drugs: receipt, dispensing, balance on hand, and prescriber details
questions.explanation: Under RA 9165 regulations, the Dangerous Drug Register (DDR) must record: date, quantity received/dispensed, prescriber name and license number, patient name, current balance. The DDR must balance the actual physical inventory. It is subject to inspection by PDEA. Discrepancies must be immediately reported.

questions.question: Which service is typically offered by a medication therapy management (MTM) pharmacy service?
choices: Filing insurance claims on behalf of patients
Comprehensive Medication Review (CMR) to identify, resolve, and prevent drug therapy problems
Free home delivery of all medications
Compounding all patient medications
questions.answer: Comprehensive Medication Review (CMR) to identify, resolve, and prevent drug therapy problems
questions.explanation: MTM services include: Comprehensive Medication Review (face-to-face or telephonic review of all medications), Targeted Medication Review (for specific conditions), medication-related action plan, intervention and referral, documentation and follow-up. Aims to optimize drug therapy outcomes, especially in elderly patients with complex medication regimens.

questions.question: The role of a clinical pharmacist during hospital 'rounds' (patient ward rounds) is to:
choices: Perform physical examination and diagnose patients
Review medication orders, identify drug therapy problems, recommend optimizations, and monitor patient response to therapy
Assist nurses with patient transfer
Manage patient bed assignments
questions.answer: Review medication orders, identify drug therapy problems, recommend optimizations, and monitor patient response to therapy
questions.explanation: During multidisciplinary ward rounds, clinical pharmacists: review all medications for appropriateness, dosing, interactions, and duplications; monitor drug efficacy and toxicity using lab values and clinical signs; recommend dose adjustments (renal/hepatic impairment); suggest therapeutic alternatives; provide drug information; and document pharmaceutical care interventions.

questions.question: When a patient is discharged from the hospital, the clinical pharmacist's role in discharge planning includes:
choices: Handling the financial billing
Providing comprehensive discharge medication counseling and reconciling discharge medications with pre-admission medications
Scheduling follow-up physician appointments only
Preparing the discharge summary exclusively
questions.answer: Providing comprehensive discharge medication counseling and reconciling discharge medications with pre-admission medications
questions.explanation: Discharge medication reconciliation ensures a complete, accurate medication list is communicated. Discharge counseling: explains new medications, discontinued medications, dose changes, administration instructions, monitoring parameters, and when to call the doctor. Prevents medication errors at care transitions, which are a leading cause of readmissions.

questions.question: In a hospital's antibiotic stewardship program, the clinical pharmacist helps to:
choices: Promote unrestricted antibiotic use for all infections
Optimize antibiotic selection, dose, duration, and route to improve outcomes and reduce resistance
Maintain a large floor stock of all antibiotics
Ensure the hospital purchases the cheapest available antibiotics
questions.answer: Optimize antibiotic selection, dose, duration, and route to improve outcomes and reduce resistance
questions.explanation: Antibiotic stewardship programs (ASPs) aim to: ensure appropriate antibiotic selection based on culture/sensitivity data, optimize PK/PD dosing, streamline/de-escalate therapy, minimize unnecessary treatment duration, and prevent Clostridioides difficile infections. Clinical pharmacists are core ASP team members who review antibiotic orders and make recommendations.

questions.question: The use of 'tall man' lettering for LASA drug names (e.g., hydrOXYzine vs. hydrALAzine) is a patient safety strategy to:
choices: Indicate the brand name of the drug
Visually differentiate similar drug names to reduce look-alike/sound-alike medication errors
Indicate the drug's potency level
Show the route of administration
questions.answer: Visually differentiate similar drug names to reduce look-alike/sound-alike medication errors
questions.explanation: Tall man lettering (mixed case) highlights the dissimilar portions of LASA drug name pairs to draw attention to the differences. Recommended by ISMP and FDA. Examples: DOPamine vs. DOBUTamine; predniSONE vs. predniSOLONE; metFORMIN vs. metronidaZOLE. Used on labels, medication administration records, and pharmacy shelving.

questions.question: In hospital pharmacy, 'IV push' administration means the drug is:
choices: Administered over 15-60 minutes via IV infusion
Given as a rapid bolus injection over 1-5 minutes directly into the IV line
Given intramuscularly with a push motion
Added to a large-volume IV bag for continuous infusion
questions.answer: Given as a rapid bolus injection over 1-5 minutes directly into the IV line
questions.explanation: IV push (IVP) or bolus: injects a small volume (usually ≤10 mL) directly into the IV line over 1-5 minutes (some over 30 seconds). Achieves rapid peak concentrations. Used for emergency drugs, furosemide, ondansetron. Must be given at the correct rate — some drugs cause cardiac arrest if given too rapidly (e.g., adenosine: very rapid; vancomycin: must be slow infusion, not IVP).

questions.question: Community pharmacy operations require a pharmacy to obtain from the FDA Philippines:
choices: Only a business permit from the local government unit
A License to Operate (LTO) from FDA in addition to local business permits
Only a certification from the Philippine Pharmacists Association
No federal license if the pharmacist holds a valid PRC license
questions.answer: A License to Operate (LTO) from FDA in addition to local business permits
questions.explanation: All pharmaceutical establishments (drugstores, hospital pharmacies, drug manufacturers, distributors) must obtain an FDA License to Operate (LTO) annually. The LTO certifies that the establishment complies with Good Distribution Practice/Good Pharmacy Practice standards. LTO is separate from the pharmacist's PRC license and local business permits.

questions.question: In a hospital, which committee is responsible for investigating and developing policies to prevent medication errors?
choices: Medical Executive Committee
Pharmacy and Therapeutics (P&T) Committee
Finance and Budget Committee
Medical Records Committee
questions.answer: Pharmacy and Therapeutics (P&T) Committee
questions.explanation: The P&T Committee (also called Pharmacy and Therapeutics or Drug Policy Committee) oversees all aspects of medication use, including: medication error investigation and prevention, high-alert medication policies, LASA drug management, formulary decisions, adverse drug event reporting, and antibiotic stewardship. It is multidisciplinary (physicians, pharmacists, nurses, administrators).
